hypothesis: If adaptive, multi-metric diversity constraints (similarity-, fragment-, and n-gram–based) guided by NaviDiv are applied during REINVENT4 reinforcement learning for singlet-fission molecules, then the generated libraries will exhibit significantly higher and more stable chemical diversity (lower mean pairwise Tanimoto similarity, more clusters, higher unique-fragment percentages, fewer overrepresented patterns) without a significant reduction in predicted ΔE_SF performance compared to baseline or fragment-only regimes, because dynamic penalization of overrepresented structures prevents mode collapse and steers exploration toward novel scaffolds.
